title: Nonclassical energy-change distribution as a witness of non-Markovian quantum dynamics
authors: Marco Pezzutto, Anton Corr, Gabriele De Chiara, Salvatore Lorenzo, Stefano Gherardini
abstract: We address the problem of identifying non-Markovian quantum time evolutions of an open quantum system by only performing measurements of the system's energy. We demonstrate that violations of CP-divisibility are always witnessed by non-positive values of the energy-change Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability distribution associated with the system's Hamiltonian, evaluated at consecutive times. The link between non CP-divisibility and non-positivity of the system's energy-change distribution is stronger when the system-environment interactions are energy-preserving. The witness works whenever anomalous energy fluxes, due to non-Markovianity, are realized. Anomalous fluxes are also detected by the non-Markovianity measure built over the quantum mutual information between the states of the open system and of a quantum correlated reference.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the non-classicality (non-positivity) of the energy-change Kirkwood-Dirac distribution serves as a robust witness for non-Markovian quantum dynamics. It shows that this witness is particularly effective when system-environment interactions preserve energy and remains valid even under anisotropic coupling.
main_problem: The paper aims to identify non-Markovian quantum dynamics in open systems using only energy measurements by investigating the non-positivity of the energy-change Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability distribution.
main_result: Violations of CP-divisibility are always witnessed by non-positive values in the energy-change Kirkwood-Dirac distribution, a link that is strongest in energy-preserving interaction regimes.
method: The authors use a collision model framework, applying matrix-element representations of qubit maps and evaluating non-positivity functionals of the Kirkwood-Dirac distribution.
model_or_system: A memory-mediated collision model consisting of a system (S), an auxiliary memory (M), and an environment (A), modeled as spin-1/2 particles interacting via Heisenberg-type exchange interactions.
key_observables: System energy changes (u_S), Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability distribution, quantum mutual information, and anomalous energy fluxes.
important_parameters: Coupling anisotropy (gamma), system-memory-environment frequencies, detuning (delta_omega), and collision number (n).
main_assumptions: The system is initialized in a thermal state, and the dynamics are assumed to be phase-covariant (z-rotation symmetry) in certain parts of the analysis.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares the non-positivity functional with non-CP-divisibility measures and energy conditions; Figure 4 shows the dependence of non-Markovianity measures (I_LFS, I_RHP) on the coupling anisotropy parameter gamma.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-Markovianity detection, defines a collision model with Heisenberg interactions, develops the mathematical framework for phase-covariant maps and Kirkwood-Dirac distributions, analyzes the impact of anisotropy on the witness, and concludes with a comparison of different non-Markovianity measures.
why_interesting: It provides a practical, measurement-efficient way to detect memory effects in open quantum systems using only local energy measurements, which is highly relevant for experimentalists in quantum optics and superconducting circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Energy-preserving interactions. Top panel: Com- parison between non-CP-divisibility identified by gn (black circles), and the non-positivity functional Nq[Pn] of the sys- tem’s energy-change distribution (brown triangles). Bottom panels: zoomed-in view of the top panel where Nq[Pn] be- comes nonzero (on the left) and returns to zero (on the right). In all panels we report the conditions (11) (red dots, green squares and blue rhombs, respectively), as well as the aver- age system’s energy-change ⟨∆ES ⟩normalized by ωS (teal triangles). FIG. 2: Energy-preserving interactions. Comparison of ∆I(n) LS (green rhombs), gn (black triangles) and Nq[Pn] (brown cir- cles), as a function of collision number. The initial states of S, M, A and model’s parameters are the same as in Fig. 1.

FIG. 3: Non energy-preserving interactions. Top panel: Com- parison between violations of CP-divisibility identified by gn (black circles), and Nq[Pn] (brown triangles). We report both the conditions (11) (red dots, green squares and blue rhombs respectively), and the average system’s energy-change ⟨∆ES ⟩ normalized by ωS (teal triangles). The system’s frequency is ωS = 0.8 (∆ω = −0.2). Bottom panel: Comparison of ILFS (green rhombs), IRHP (orange triangles) and P n Nq[Pn] (light blue circles), as a function of detuning ∆ω ∈[−0.5, 0.5] (keep- ing memory and environment particles at the same frequency). Here, the maximum number of collisions is fixed to 103. All the other parameters are the...

FIG. 4: Comparison of ILFS (green rhombs), IRHP (orange triangles) and P n Nq[Pn] (light blue circles), as a function of the coupling anisotropy γ ∈[−1, 1] for system-memory collisions. The interaction between memory and environment particles is kept energy-preserving. Moreover, the maximum number of collisions is fixed to 103. All the other parameters are the same as in Fig. 1.
